zn_pi.name: Ziegler and Nichols [8] - Process reaction (Page 23)
zn_pi.advantages: Simple to calculate, ideal for heavily lag-dominant processes. Provides highly aggressive setpoint tracking.
zn_pi.disadvantages: Designed for a quarter-decay ratio, inherently causing ~25% overshoot. Highly unstable if the dead-time ratio approaches 1.0.

cc_pi.name: Cohen and Coon [11] - process reaction (Page 23)
cc_pi.advantages: Incorporates dead time directly into the mathematical structure. Better load rejection for processes where delay is significant.
cc_pi.disadvantages: High proportional gain leads to aggressive valve action and unavoidable mechanical oscillation.

chien_reg_0.name: Chien et al. [10] - regulator (Page 23)
chien_reg_0.advantages: Optimized specifically to reject load disturbances without causing the process to overshoot the setpoint (0% overshoot).
chien_reg_0.disadvantages: The conservative mathematics result in a slightly more sluggish recovery time compared to heuristic methods.

itae_reg.name: Minimum ITAE - Murrill [13] (Page 25)
itae_reg.advantages: Mathematically minimizes the Integral of Time-weighted Absolute Error for load changes. Eliminates slow, rolling oscillations.
itae_reg.disadvantages: Complex exponential math requires precise modeling. Very sensitive to inaccuracies in the initial process parameters.

zn_pid.name: Ziegler and Nichols [8] - Process reaction (Page 69)
zn_pid.advantages: Fastest heuristic response available. Uses derivative action to dampen the oscillations caused by aggressive proportional settings.
zn_pid.disadvantages: Highly sensitive to sensor noise. High derivative gain can cause severe valve wear and chatter. Still prone to overshoot.

cc_pid.name: Cohen and Coon [11] - process reaction (Page 69)
cc_pid.advantages: Excellent load rejection for processes with significant delay, combining dead-time logic with predictive derivative action.
cc_pid.disadvantages: Generates a very active control signal. Can lead to aggressive valve action and wear on mechanical components.
